revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:場所請負制]][[zh:场所请负制]]
'''Hashiyoke Oisei''' (場所請負制) is a distribution system unique to the {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|qid=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} that evolved from the merchant (basho) chigyō system ({{ill|Local chigyō (Basho chigyō system)|ja|地方知行#商場知行制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Local chigyō (Basho chigyō system)|1=jikata chigyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424398}}) of retainers under the rule of the {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|qid=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. [[File:Scenes of the Ezo Fishing Grounds by Kodama Teiryo (Art Gallery of South Australia).jpg|450px|thumb|{{ill|Kodama Teiryo|ja|小玉貞良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodama Teiryo|1=Kodama Teiryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q27921158}}’s {{ill|Ainu genre painting|en|Ainu genre painting|qid=Q11282352|lt=Ainu painting|1=Ainu genre painting}} depicting Japanese and Ainu at a trading site]] == Background of its Emergence == During the Edo period, the agricultural techniques available made it difficult to cultivate {{ill|rice|ja|稲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rice|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イネ|1=Oryza sativa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q161426}} in cold regions. In the areas ruled by the {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|qid=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}} in {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|qid=Q1040184|lt=Ezochi|1=Ezo}}, the local geography precluded an expectation of a rice harvest. Consequently, the stipends ({{ill|fōroku|ja|俸禄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fōroku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禄|1=fōroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6854104}}) granted by the domain lord ({{ill|han‐shu|ja|藩主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/han‐shu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 藩|1=Han system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q841985}}) to his retainers were not based on rice yield ({{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|qid=Q909302|lt=sekidaka|1=Kokudaka}}) and the conventional local chigyō system ({{ill|Local chigyō|ja|地方知行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Local chigyō|1=jikata chigyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424398}}), but instead were established through the so-called merchant (basho) chigyō system. Under this system, trading sites – which functioned as the equivalent of land grants ({{ill|kyūchi|ja|給地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kyūchi|1=kyūchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11606756}}) – were set up in the fishing grounds and in the trading regions with the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}}, outside the storehouse lands ({{ill|Kuranirushi|ja|蔵入地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuranirushi|1=kurairichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11621880}}), and the rights to conduct trade there were allocated to retainers as their chigyō. In the land grant areas of the Japanese ({{ill|Wajin|ja|和人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajin|qq=}}) territories, the right to collect in‐kind taxes from fishermen was granted in a manner almost identical to the local chigyō system. However, since most of the Wajin territory consisted of the Matsumae Domain’s storehouse lands, the majority of retainers’ land grants were located in Ezochi. Moreover, within these grants all rights – such as mining, falconry (waiting for hawks), salmon and trout fishing, logging, and so forth – belonged to the domain lord. The only privilege accorded to the chigyō lords was the right to outfit a boat at their own expense once a year for trading purposes. Under these conditions, merchants with ample capital – such as the {{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – began to establish shops in {{ill|Matsumae, Hokkaido|en|Matsumae, Hokkaido|qid=Q1374364|lt=Matsumae|1=Matsumae, Hokkaido}} and made a full-scale entry into the market. The retainers holding chigyō would borrow trading supplies and even living expenses from these merchants to engage in trade, later handing over the goods obtained to the merchants for reimbursement. However, as trade in Ezochi grew increasingly complex and the samurai found themselves unable to manage it both financially and technologically, resulting in mounting debts, the system gradually shifted in the early {{ill|18th century|en|18th century|qid=Q7015|lt=18th century|1=18th century}}. Trading rights themselves came to be handled by merchants under the title of {{ill|Basho ukeoirinin|ja|場所請負人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Basho ukeoirinin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 場所請負制|1=Bashoukeoisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428085}}, with the chigyō lords receiving a fixed sum of {{ill|unjōkin|ja|運上金|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/unjōkin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 運上|1=unjōkin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11640406}}. This transformation marked the establishment of the Hashiyoke Oisei. === Administration after the Establishment of the Hashiyoke Oisei System === [[File:Ainu-iomante-bear-spirit-sending-ceremony-by-Hirasawa-Byozan-1875.png|450px|thumb|{{ill|Iomante|en|Iomante|qid=Q583794|lt=Iomante|1=Iomante}} celebration. Villagers revel in feasts and dance, while a Japanese man presumed to be the site supervisor sits in the prime seat before the altar. (From an Ainu painting by {{ill|Hirasawa Byozan|ja|平沢屏山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirasawa Byozan|1=Hirasawa Byōzan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4497851}}, 1875.)]] {{See2|For information on Edo period villages, see also {{ill|Mura uke seido|en|Mura uke seido|qid=Q11523540|lt=Village contracting system|1=Mura uke seido}} and {{ill|Kotan (village)|en|Kotan (village)|qid=Q11302190|lt=Kotan|1=Kotan (village)}}, as well as details on the social classes ({{ill|Four occupations|en|Four occupations|qid=Q1328489|lt=Shi-nō-kōshō|1=Four occupations}}).|qq=}} At that time, the administration of regions such as {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|qid=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|qid=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin|1=Sakhalin}}, and the {{ill|Northern Territories|en|Northern Territories|qid=Q1217330|lt=Northern Territories|1=Northern Territories}} was generally organized on the basis of chigyō areas (trading sites), and a village system similar to that of areas south of {{ill|Honshu|en|Honshu|qid=Q13989|lt=Honshu|1=Honshu}} was implemented. In documents concerning chigyō areas, terms such as “{{ill|basho|ja|場所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basho|1=basho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428084}}” as well as “{{ill|ryō|ja|領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ryō|1=ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11664885}}” are used. The basho ukeoirinin exercised administrative authority in place of the chigyō lords. In addition, the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}} were classified as peasants ({{ill|Baixing|en|Baixing|qid=Q843939|lt=hyakushō|1=Baixing}}). In the {{ill|Omusha|en|Omusha|qid=Q11292412|lt=Omusha|1=Omusha}}, provisions such as medicine and emergency rice (assistance) were supplied to the elderly, the sick, and children, and local influential figures were appointed to roles known collectively as {{ill|Yakuezo|ja|役蝦夷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakuezo|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 地方三役|1=jikata san'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11424333}} (including positions such as {{ill|Sōotona|ja|惣乙名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōotona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庄屋|1=Nanushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110095960}}, {{ill|Otona|ja|乙名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名主|1=myōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110095935}}, {{ill|Wakaiotona|ja|脇乙名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaiotona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 庄屋|1=Nanushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110095960}}, {{ill|Sōkozu|ja|惣小使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōkozu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 組頭|1=Kumigashira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11606351}}, {{ill|Kogami|ja|小使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 組頭|1=Kumigashira|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11606351}}, and {{ill|Toshiagetori|ja|土産取|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshiagetori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 百姓代|1=hyakushōdai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q61057573}})<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=榎森進 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1204/00024166/ |title=「日露和親条約」調印後の幕府の北方地域政策について (榎森進教授退任記念号) |journal=東北学院大学論集. 歴史と文化 |ISSN=1880-8425 |publisher=東北学院大学学術研究会 |year=2014 |issue=52 |pages=17-37,39,41,43 |naid=120006893046|qq=}}</ref>. These officials not only transmitted the edicts ({{ill|Statute|en|Statute|qid=Q820655|lt=laws|1=Statute}}) issued by the domain and the shogunate to the common Ainu ({{ill|Hira Ainu|ja|平蝦夷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hira Ainu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), but also conducted censuses (the creation of registers as described in {{ill|Sōmon Ninbetsu Kaichō|ja|宗門人別改帳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōmon Ninbetsu Kaichō|1=shūmon ninbetsu aratame chō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452068}}, i.e. {{ill|koseki|ja|戸籍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/koseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Family register|qid=Q485228}}; see also {{ill|Population statistics of Edo Japan|ja|江戸時代の日本の人口統計|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Population statistics of Edo Japan|1=demographics of Edo-period Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17219274}}), collected local products such as animal skins in lieu of {{ill|nenko|ja|年貢米|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nenko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 年貢|1=nengu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483871}}, and managed the mobilization of labor for various tasks ({{ill|oyaku|ja|夫役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oyaku|1=buyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11444785}}), including duties at the {{ill|Kaisho|ja|会所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisho|1=Kaisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381200}}, the {{ill|Unjōya|ja|運上屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unjōya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 場所請負制|1=Bashoukeoisei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428085}}, or other miscellaneous tasks performed by the {{ill|Banya|ja|番屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Banya|1=ban'ya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578756}}. Furthermore, with regard to Ainu fishing ({{ill|gyorō|ja|漁撈|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/gyorō|1=gyorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11565346}}), various forms existed such as employment-based fishing labor and self-employment (Ainu engaging in their own trade). At that time, even in Japanese society systems such as the parasitic landlord system ({{ill|Kiseichishusei|ja|寄生地主制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiseichishusei|1=Kiseichishusei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q48743678}}) as well as indentured servitude – including apprenticeships ({{ill|Tingchi Hōkō|ja|丁稚奉公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tingchi Hōkō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 丁稚|1=apprentice|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11353322}}) and live-in domestic service ({{ill|Sumikomi Josei|ja|住み込み女中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumikomi Josei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) as part of the broader system of {{ill|Indentured servitude|en|Indentured servitude|qid=Q1122364|lt=Nenki Hōkō|1=Indentured servitude}} – were common, and life was by no means easy. Under the rule of the {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|qid=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}}, the use of native Japanese language ({{ill|Wago|ja|和語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wago|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大和言葉|1=Wago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1022155}}) and traditional Japanese dress ({{ill|Wasō|ja|和装|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wasō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 和服|1=Kimono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q483444}}) were prohibited; however, during the administration under the Hakodate Bugyō ({{ill|Hakodate bugyō|en|Hakodate bugyō|qid=Q5640582|lt=Hakodate Bugyō|1=Hakodate bugyō}} / {{ill|Matsumae Bugyō|ja|松前奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumae Bugyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 遠国奉行|1=Ongoku-bugyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17171698}} / {{ill|Ezo Bugyō|ja|蝦夷奉行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ezo Bugyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 遠国奉行|1=Ongoku-bugyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17171698}}) these restrictions were lifted and even promoted as part of a policy of Japaneseization ({{ill|Wafūka|ja|和風化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wafūka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Those adopting Japanese dress were rewarded with items such as clothing and pomade. This Japaneseization was carried out primarily by the yakuezo, and did not spread widely among the common Ainu ({{ill|Hira Ainu|ja|平蝦夷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hira Ainu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=遠藤匡俊 |url=http://id.nii.ac.jp/1399/00012639/ |title=根室場所におけるアイヌの命名規則と幕府の同化政策 |journal=歴史地理学 |ISSN=03887464 |publisher=歴史地理学会 |year=2002 |month=jan |volume=44 |issue=1 |pages=48-59 |naid=120005399706|qq=}}</ref> The rate of Japaneseization varied by region, with some officials in charge of managing the trading sites either not actively pursuing it or, in some cases, implementing it rather forcefully. Moreover, from the period following the Second Bakuryō, as a measure against smallpox prevalent in Ezochi, residents were also inoculated.<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=松木明知 |year=2010-09 |title=新出の平沢屏山のアイヌ種痘図に関する一考察 : オムスク造形美術館所蔵の「種痘図」を巡って |url=http://jsmh.umin.jp/journal/56-3/56-3_427.pdf |format=PDF |journal=日本醫史學雜誌 |publisher=日本医史学会 |volume=56 |issue=3 |pages=427-436 |naid=10026700614 |ISSN=05493323|qq=}}</ref><ref>諸澄邦彦 [https://www.jrias.or.jp/books/pdf/201407_IRYOUSHISEKI_MOROZUMI.pdf Medical History Sites – Gorōji and Hisuzō] Isotope News, July 2014, No.723</ref><ref>[https://www.wul.waseda.ac.jp/kotenseki/html/chi05/chi05_04217/index.html "Illustration of Ainu Smallpox Inoculation" by Kunisada, Waseda University]</ref><ref>[http://archives.c.fun.ac.jp/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_01/shishi_03-05/shishi_03-05-13-03-09~11.htm "Promotion of Smallpox Inoculation, as a Measure against Edema Disease" – Hakodate City Digital History]</ref> == History == * In the {{ill|1700s|ja|1700th year 代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1700s|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, under the supervision of the {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|qid=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}}, the transition to the basho ukeoi system advanced. * In {{ill|1774|en|1774|qid=Q7697|lt=1774|1=1774}} ({{ill|An'ei|ja|安永|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 安永 (元号)|1=An'ei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1193910}} 2), due to the debt restructuring of 5400 ryo undertaken by the domain lord {{ill|Matsumae Michihiro|ja|松前道広|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumae Michihiro|1=Matsumae Michihiro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8514314}}, four fishing grounds were converted to the contracting system. * In {{ill|1789|en|1789|qid=Q7772|lt=1789|1=1789}} ({{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|qid=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei|1=Tenmei}} 8), the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|qid=Q3454489|lt=Kunashiri-Menashi Battle|1=Menashi–Kunashir rebellion}} (also known as the Kansei Ezo uprising or Kansei Ezo Rebellion) occurred. This uprising broke out during the absence of the Ainu village head ({{ill|shōya|ja|乙名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shōya|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 名主|1=myōshu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q110095935}}) and was driven by the Ainu’s growing dissatisfaction with the conditions in commercial transactions and at the fishing grounds (see also {{ill|Ishūretsuzō|en|Ishūretsuzō|qid=Q11444835|lt=Eisho Retsuzō|1=Ishūretsuzō}}). * In {{ill|1795|en|1795|qid=Q7815|lt=1795|1=1795}} ({{ill|Kansei|en|Kansei|qid=Q1195131|lt=Kansei|1=Kansei}} 7), in pursuit of trade, {{ill|Adam Laxman|en|Adam Laxman|qid=Q29787|lt=Adam Laxman|1=Adam Laxman}} arrived in {{ill|Nemuro Province|en|Nemuro Province|qid=Q1063001|lt=Nemuro|1=Nemuro Province}}, heightening tensions over Russia’s southern expansion policy. * In {{ill|1799|en|1799|qid=Q7827|lt=1799|1=1799}}, the eastern part of Ezochi (the Pacific coast of Ezochi) was provisionally designated as {{ill|Kamichi|ja|上知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamichi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 上知令|1=Kamichi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q702796}} and became {{ill|tenryō|ja|公議御料|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tenryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天領|1=Tenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1685411}} (territory directly controlled by the shogunate). In addition to the shogunate conducting relief trade with the Ainu, the basho ukeoi system in eastern Ezochi was converted to a direct management system, with shogunate officials ({{ill|bakumon|ja|幕臣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bakumon|1=bakushin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481849}}) present during transactions to prevent irregularities. * In {{ill|1806|en|1806|qid=Q6903|lt=1806|1=1806}} ({{ill|Culture|en|Culture|qid=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}} 3), western Ezochi was made into {{ill|tenryō|ja|公議御料|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tenryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天領|1=Tenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1685411}}. All of Ezochi became territory directly controlled by the shogunate, and the basho ukeoi system was abolished in eastern Ezochi. In western Ezochi, however, it continued under the supervision of shogunate officials. * In {{ill|1812|en|1812|qid=Q6933|lt=1812|1=1812}}, for reasons including cost reduction, the shogunate decided to reinstate the basho ukeoi system<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_01/shishi_03-03/shishi_03-03-07-01-01.htm|title=Reasons for the Abolition of the Direct Management System|work=Hakodate City History|publisher=Hakodate City|date=|accessdate=2014-12-13|qq=}}</ref>. From the following year, an auction system was introduced and improvements in the treatment of Ainu labor ({{ill|oyaku|ja|夫役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oyaku|1=buyaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11444785}}) and related benefits were established. * In {{ill|1813|en|1813|qid=Q6937|lt=1813|1=1813}}, the basho ukeoi system was reinstated in 19 locations<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.lib-hkd.jp/hensan/hakodateshishi/tsuusetsu_01/shishi_03-03/shishi_03-03-07-01-04.htm|title=Change of Basho Ukeoi Personnel|work=Hakodate City History|publisher=Hakodate City|date=|accessdate=2014-12-13|qq=}}</ref>. * In {{ill|1821|en|1821|qid=Q6976|lt=1821|1=1821}} ({{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|qid=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei|1=Bunsei}} 4), the Matsumae Domain regained its territories, with administrative authority shifting back from the shogunate to the domain. Following the death of the domain lord {{ill|Matsumae Akihiro|en|Matsumae Akihiro|qid=Q8514326|lt=Matsumae Akihiro|1=Matsumae Akihiro}} in 1833, the domain’s discipline deteriorated and the power of the contracting personnel increased once again. * In {{ill|1855|en|1855|qid=Q7673|lt=1855|1=1855}} ({{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|qid=Q1146125|lt=Kaei|1=Kaei}} 7), during the second Bakuryō period, Ezochi once again became {{ill|tenryō|ja|公議御料|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tenryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天領|1=Tenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1685411}} (territory directly controlled by the shogunate). The lands of the Matsumae Domain were then limited to a portion of the Japanese ({{ill|Wajin|ja|和人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wajin|qq=}}) territories. Regions designated as tenryō returned to shogunate supervision, and oversight of the contracting personnel was strengthened. * In {{ill|1868|en|1868|qid=Q7717|lt=1868|1=1868}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 1), the system was abolished by the {{ill|Hokkaidō Development Commission|en|Hokkaidō Development Commission|qid=Q524428|lt=Kaitakushi|1=Hokkaidō Development Commission}}. == End of the System == The basho ukeoi system was explicitly abolished in September of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 2 by {{ill|Shima Yoshitake|en|Shima Yoshitake|qid=Q970410|lt=Shima Yoshiyū|1=Shima Yoshitake}} of the Kaitakushi. However, due to opposition from the contracting personnel, its name was changed in October of the same year to '''Gyobamochi''' (漁場持), and it continued for a time in the former eastern Ezochi (the Pacific coast and the Kuril Islands) and in the former western Ezochi north of {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|qid=Q1134336|lt=Masu County|1=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} (the Japan Sea and Sea of Okhotsk coasts). This development led the disillusioned {{ill|Matsuura Takeshirō|en|Matsuura Takeshirō|qid=Q11531839|lt=Matsuura Takeshirō|1=Matsuura Takeshirō}} to resign his post. From December of Meiji 4 to February of Meiji 5, following the abolition of the Hakodate branch of territorial administration in Hokkaido ({{ill|Hokkaido bunryō shihai|ja|北海道の分領支配|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido bunryō shihai|1=Hokkaido bunryō shihai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11402435}}), reappointment of Gyobamochi took place; however, in the former branch territories in the regions south of {{ill|Ishikari Province|en|Ishikari Province|qid=Q1063877|lt=Ishikari Province|1=Ishikari Province}}, Gyobamochi were not established. The Gyobamochi persisted until their abolition in September of Meiji 9. == Unjōya == Also known as Unjōka, the Unjōya were established by the basho ukeoi personnel in 85 locations throughout Ezochi ({{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|qid=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|qid=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin|1=Sakhalin}}, and the {{ill|Northern Territories|en|Northern Territories|qid=Q1217330|lt=Northern Territories|1=Northern Territories}}). In each location, a supervisor, {{ill|tsūben|ja|通弁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsūben|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 通訳|1=Language interpretation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q210439}}, clerks, and guards were stationed, and policies for the welfare of the residents along with administration of the {{ill|Omusha|en|Omusha|qid=Q11292412|lt=Omusha|1=Omusha}} were implemented. Additionally, at each fishing ground, a {{ill|banya|ja|番屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/banya|1=ban'ya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11578756}} was established and staffed with guards. Initially set up as bases for trade, the Unjōya eventually came to manage fishing grounds as well, functioning as post stations (lodgings and transportation hubs) and as branch offices of the {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|qid=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}}. When Ezochi was designated as {{ill|tenryō|ja|公儀御料|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tenryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天領|1=Tenryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1685411}}, they were called {{ill|Kaisho|ja|会所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaisho|1=Kaisho|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381200}}. Shortly before the {{ill|Taisei Hōkan|ja|大政奉還|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taisei Hōkan|qq=}}, these Unjōya were abolished and became branch offices of the Hakodate Bugyō (the post referred to as the "honjin"). In the former Unjōya of Yōichi (in {{ill|Shiribeshi Province|en|Shiribeshi Province|qid=Q522521|lt=Shiribeshi Province|1=Shiribeshi Province}} and {{ill|Yoichi District, Hokkaido|en|Yoichi District, Hokkaido|qid=Q1185927|lt=Yoichi District|1=Yoichi District, Hokkaido}}), remnants of the old lower Yōichi Unjōya still remain. == Sources == {{Reflist|qq=}} == References == * {{Cite book|和書|author=函館市史編さん室, 函館市 |title=函館市史 |series=通説編 第1巻 |publisher=函館市 |year=1974 |NCID=BN01157761 |doi=10.11501/9490805 |id={{全国書誌番号|80022789|qq=}} |url=https://iss.ndl.go.jp/books/R100000039-I002636872-00 |ref={{harvid|函館市史.通説編1|qq=}} |qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Hokkaido (Rēisei)|ja|北海道 (令制)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido
